comments.title: Complete Old English Poems and World Tree Reading Request
comments.content:
Good Evening,

To those who celebrate it, a very Merry Christmas and/or Boxing Day. For everyone, I hope you're keeping warm and putting a satisfying cap on the year.

To Share: I got as a Christmas gift The Complete Old English Poems, translated by Craig Williamson with an introduction by Tom Shippey. It's exactly what the title says: the entire corpus of Old English poems translated into modern English (including, of course, Beowulf). From the bit of reading I've done so far, it looks like the translations are rendered at least mostly in the alliterative style of Old English poetry. Most of the poems are Christian, given who was literate in the Anglo-Saxon period, but there's also a lot of material for those interested in Anglo-Saxon flavored Heathenry. It also is an attractive weighty tome that will take up a satisfying amount of shelf space.

To Ask: For JMG and/or the commentariat: any recommendations for sources that discuss the idea of a "World Tree" a la the Yggdrasil of Norse myth? I have lots of material on the Germanic side of things, but I wonder if there's any interesting comparative reading to do, and I'd be thankful for any avenues to explore.

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone here for your time, care, and wisdom.

To any who will have them, I put forth my blessings and best wishes,
Jeff

Aztec mythology has four celestial trees located at the north, south, east and west corners of the world and the First Tree of the World is from Mayan mythology. comments.title: A practical question
comments.content:
Let's suppose I wanted to improve my financial situation. There are mundane means of doing it, which involve planning, basically.

But how can I use magic to help it? The advice I found so far falls into two categories:

1. Summon spirits and/or demons to bring you wealth, no strings attached, pinky promise.

2. New thought and creative visualization. Just imagine yourself rich, already possessing all you want.

How would you advice to go about it, JMG? It is a question that many would like to know an answer to, I'm sure.

P.S. Belatedly I wish you a Happy Winter Solstice, Alban Arthuan. I hope you enjoyed it.

replies.title: Re: A practical question
replies.content:
Neither one of those is an effective method. Going to a demon for help is exactly the same as going to a loan shark for help -- yeah, you'll get help, but the cost is always going to end up being much, much more than you want to pay. Creative visualization will only help you if your problem is a habit of negative imagination, and that's not very common these days -- largely because of the popularity of creative imagination!

Financial planning, all by itself, won't do you much good, either. It's a good idea, but it's hopelessly insufficient. You need more money, not just less waste of the money you have.

No, an effective magical method to become wealthy focuses on finding opportunities to earn money. That's the difference between people who start out poor and get rich, and people who start out poor and stay there: the people who get rich are always looking for opportunities to earn more money. You can use affirmations to get your mind primed to spot opportunities -- "I see opportunities to earn money all around me" repeated fifty or a hundred times daily, like a mantra, will get your subconscious going. Then make a conscious effort to follow through, and when you spot an opportunity that you can reasonably pursue, do it. (That's the place where most people fall flat -- they learn to spot opportunities and then just daydream them away.)

It's easy to become as wealthy as you want to be. All you have to do is focus your entire will on that one goal, and take concrete actions every single day to bring it about. If anything gets in the way of that goal, set it aside for now. If you make a buck, think, "How can I use this dollar to make more money?" -- and then do it. Do this day in and day out, and you'll be wealthy far sooner than you can imagine.

replies.title: Re: A practical question
replies.content:
„ That's the place where most people fall flat -- they learn to spot opportunities and then just daydream them away.“

May I ask for your advice and help with this. I have recently spotted an opportunity to get my family out of a financial bind, but as you say I literally daydream hours away on imagining what it would look like and not doing the research, training and building required to put it into action.

replies.title: Re: A practical question
replies.content:
Only you can make the decision to do something instead of sitting on your rump daydreaming. There are no tricks, no easy ways, and nothing that anyone else can do for you. If you want to make it happen, stop daydreaming and do something about it. If you don't want to make it happen -- if you'd rather just stay in your current situation -- why, by all means keep doing as you're doing.

Your will is the most irreducibly personal thing about you. It's the one thing nobody else can affect without your permission. It's thus your greatest strength, and your greatest weakness. If you want it to be a strength, be honest about it -- will what you will, will it with all your heart, and let it follow its natural course into action. If you want it to be a weakness, don't be honest with yourself -- insist that you want things but don't let yourself do anything to bring them about. The choice is entirely yours.

replies.title: Re: A practical question
replies.content: Yes. The best set of exercises I've encountered for getting a grounding in self-knowledge, especially if you have self-esteem issues, is the one taught by the Order of Spiritual Alchemy, one of the traditions I inherited from John Gilbert. (I'm delighted to say that the order is up and running again at this point.) The preliminary exercises, and the eight levels of the Octagon Society work that follows, are both free of charge and can be accessed directly from the website just linked. The OSA work is done by way of directed journaling; it takes a little time every day, and I have yet to see anyone go through it without some painful emotional turmoil, because it involves dredging up unfinished business from your past. That said, it did me a world of good, and I know a lot of other people who have benefited dramatically from doing the work.

replies.title: Re: A practical question
replies.content:
Do you want to be rich per se or do you desire wealth? There is a huge, gaping difference between riches and wealth. Health is wealth, and I have often remarked that a bottle of $700 champagne is useless if you're so unhealthy that you can only painfully drink it through a straw held up by your home nurse.

I plan on writing an essay in the next few weeks that deals with the concept of unearned wealth. For instance, the computer I am typing this message on is some of the unearned wealth in my own life. I did not mine the ores for it, I certainly did not assemble it, and I am not running on a treadmill to provide the electrical power for it. My argument is that unearned wealth and the fascination with it can be extremely toxic, especially when you aren't seeing any of the areas where the desire for it influences your life.

Instead of visualizing "all you want", perhaps visualize what you already have and then fill those objects, people, and places with your gratitude for them. Gratitude has weird powers that will never be understood by me or any other human. When you are grateful, it tends to sublimate (to make sublime) what you are grateful for by the power of seven. Meaning that the energy of your gratitude comes back at you in seven different forms at least. Oddly enough I believe gratitude has the power to reach into the past and do things to it, though that's a concept I have not yet meditated on enough to comment intelligently about. When you cultivate gratitude, suddenly what you need (but not necessarily all you want) falls into your lap and your needs are mysteriously met.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
One of the problems I have with metta practice is that it conflicts with the free will of the people it tries to influence. Not everyone wants to experience unexpected success -- quite the contrary, many people are committed to lives of failure and will fight like wildcats if you try to get them out of their ruts. Furthermore, they have the right to the life they choose -- and it may well be that they have good karmic reasons to make that choice. So, no, I don't recommend that at all.

If you want to work with something like "Everyone who wants unexpected success experiences it" or something like that, that would be an interesting experiment. Will it succeed? Why, that's one of the interesting questions it might help settle.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Ah, ok, thank you. That brings up another question I had, though: Last week, in answer to a question about the Essene healing hands technique, you said "There may be people you know who will give their consent to receiving healing energy; you can also direct the energy to heal larger things -- your city, your country, your species, the planet. (If you belong to any of these you have the right to heal it.)" How is it different to direct healing energy towards our species or the planet rather than to something like "everyone"? (Since you've raised the same ethical issue of not directing healing to people who don't want it as you have just mentioned now about affirmations.) I know the words are different, but the concepts seem like the same thing to me. Or if by healing the species you mean something like healing the relationships between people or healing the functioning of the species as a whole rather than healing people individually themselves, why would the same ethical issue not apply?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Healing the species or the planet involves working with the whole, not with individuals. You can do healing work for your family, your community, your nation, your species, and your planet because you're part of them -- in effect, you're a citizen and so you have a vote. You're not forcing any individual member of the family, community, nation, etc. to make the changes you think they should make; instead, you're casting your vote regarding the state of the whole.

"Everyone," by contrast, literally means every individual, so you're trespassing on their free will. If you want to say "My species experiences unexpected success," that's different, since the species can experience something without every individual experiencing it.

replies.title: Re: Alchemy, Dolmen Arch, Bardon
replies.content:
1) That should be no problem. I don't recommend learning two new systems at the same time, but maintaining one by going back through the training sequence, while learning the other -- that's something I've done tolerably often, and in fact will be doing again in the new year. Bardon's system is not one I recommend for beginners, but if you're finishing up the Dolmen Arch you're emphatically not a beginner and should get a lot of benefit out of Bardon's methods.

replies.title: Re: The winter storm

Some parts north of the US border got positively slammed by the pre-Christmas storm (i.e., the corridor from Windsor, Ontario to Quebec City, Quebec). Power lost in parts of Quebec for several days now. 400 km of freeway closed for two days due to extensive white-outs and hundred-vehicle smash-ups. Passengers trapped in stationary trains out-in-the-middle-of-nowhere for 24 hours or more without food or functioning lavatory facilities. A six-foot snow drift blocking my sister’s farm-house front door (worst storm in her 45 years on the farm, she told me today). In terms of scale, Canada’s role in bringing cold and darkness to a particular portion of Eastern Europe may be small compared to the US, but the difference is merely a matter of degree not kind. Ron M

replies.content:
If JMG will allow,

Having been at the receiving end of something very similar I tried different "remedies" some of which obviously did some good and others less so.

Here are a few of those that helped, in no particular order:

1-Completely cleansing the space when the particular individual(s) are absent, and continuing doing so on a regular basis (do this before laying out the consecrated salt and the vinegar).

2-Burning incense, frankincense or myrrh, while praying to Saint Michael the archangel. Also try lighting a candle while praying and letting it burn itself out afterwards, or extinguish it without blowing on it. Praying is very important and should be done at least twice a day, more often if at all possible. The prayers can be short but must be meaningful and heartfelt, the intentions must be clear. Here is a link to the Wikipedia page on St-Michael's prayer, which could be a good starting point https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prayer_to_Saint_Michael. Although you are not a christian, the christian God is, contrary to popular beliefs, a rather friendly and understanding God and if you decide to pray to him as well It might prove helpful. Very important, invoking is good but showing gratitude to Saint-Michael is equally as important, archangels cannot be commanded to do something for us.

3-Using consecrated salt in the space, at different locations in small receptacles and changing it on a regular basis. First time use of no more than 5-7 days, less so if things are very nasty. Afterwards you can prolong the "use" time as the situation gets better. Dispose of the used up salt in the toilet, making sure it ends up in the main sewer, and if you have a septic system dispose of the salt in a running stream.

4-Placing small bowls of household vinegar in discrete locations throughout the space. Replenish when required.

5-Playing Gregorian music, real Gregorian music, in Latin, no instruments, and from an original source (CD, record, cassette, tape) if at all possible, if it is not possible to do so streaming should do but try to make sure it is real Gregorian music, not the new style stuff.

6-Roll up the shades, open up the curtains, bring in the light! Make the space airy, bright and welcoming! Those that are kind of obsessed or possessed hate the light but it will do wonders to the space's energy and might help neuter the entity involved. The more unwelcoming the environment to negative entities and energies the more helpful it will be to clear out the the entity.

7-Do not forget that the entity/ies are not be allowed in your space and let them know in uncertain terms that is the case, by saying so and trough prayer. The Saint-Michael prayer in Latin seems to be quite disliked by the evil entities.

8-Suggestions as per JMG's Magic Monday FAQ and his Encyclopedia of Natural Magic.

All of these should have and effect on the negative energies and entities that are probably present in the space: be aware they might irritate the person(s) at first, but after a while will render the environment unwelcoming to the negative entities and kind of "syphon off" some of their energies rendering them less powerful thus having a weaker influence. In my personal experience, it made the space less heavily charged, less murky, quieted down the person involved, and got things to be just barely acceptable at the worst of times and pleasurable at the best of times, but it took a while and the measures must be done on a regular basis. As an additional benefit all of this might afford a certain level of protection to the other individuals involved and those visiting the space.

Hope this proves helpful, good luck and may Saint-Michael help and protect you in your endeavours.

Sapienter Si Sincere.

comments.title: magic, simplicity, and complexity
comments.content:
I was reading a book about solomonic magic. It was interesting, but I was struck at how involved and complicated doing its operations were as far as assembling materials and conducting rituals.

Nothing against such an approach, but due to my busy life as teacher and parent, I don't have the time or energy to put all that time into magic practice.

Now for a couple question.

1) To what extent is more better as far as doing rituals and connecting with gods and angels?

2) I'm curious if you might share your thoughts in general about Solomonic approaches to magic.

3) I know it's a huge question, and I gather that these days it's not your magical focus, but could you please make some suggestions for praying to/working with/learning from angels and archangels? I have finished Learning Ritual Magic but have a 2 year old and thus need let Circles of Power go for now and be simple in my approach until she is older.

Thank you,

Puce Elongated Ungulate

replies.title: Re: magic, simplicity, and complexity
replies.content:
Okay, I'm going to preface this by saying that I don't practice Solomonic magic; I've read all the classic texts, of course, but it's not a system I work with. It's also not a system I like. Keep that in mind, remember your mileage may vary, etc.

My take is that Solomonic magic is a method of making spirits do magical work for you because you're not willing to do it for yourself. To my mind, that's a very bad idea. It makes you dependent on the spirits, and even if they don't happen to be malicious -- as many of them are -- they don't have your best interests in mind, and they can and will mess with you.

All that hardware and ornate ritual? That's what you have to do to get effects, again, if you're not willing to put in the effort of daily practice and of changing your own consciousness to change your workd. The clutter is unnecessary if you learn how to work directly with consciousness and magical energy, which again, you can learn through the right kinds of daily practice. I used to do Golden Dawn magic, which pound for pound has as much hardware and ornate ritual as any magical system in the world. I got good results with it, don't get me wrong -- but I get better results now, with much less hardware and much simpler workings.

As for working with angels and archangels, if you've got a two-year-old in the house, your best approach is prayer. That doesn't involve any hardware at all, just a simple willingness to interact with spiritual beings. If you have a copy handy, my book Paths of Wisdom has a discussion of prayer in it you may find useful.

replies.title: Re: magic, simplicity, and complexity
replies.content:
"My take is that Solomonic magic is a method of making spirits do magical work for you because you're not willing to do it for yourself. To my mind, that's a very bad idea. It makes you dependent on the spirits, and even if they don't happen to be malicious -- as many of them are -- they don't have your best interests in mind, and they can and will mess with you."

Well, how exactly does it make the spirits do the work? Does it rise to the level of attempting to turn spirits into slaves? If so, it makes perfect sense that even the most friendly spirits would mess with anyone who attempts to use that system...

replies.title: Re: magic, simplicity, and complexity
replies.content: Yes, very much so. Most Solomonic evocations are methods of bullying and browbeating spirits to make them do what you want. If they don't show up on command there are ways of inflicting pain on the spirits to make them obey. That's one reason I have zero interest in the system -- the Druid and Rosicrucian traditions I've studied focus on establishing friendly and mutually beneficial relationships with spiritual beings, and that's always worked very well for me.

replies.content:
a) Does the Golden Dawn system lean more towards the Rosicrucian methods?

b) You've mentioned before that spirits can help us in the afterlife. Are they also able to hurt us in the afterlife? What sort of effects could this have?

replies.title: Re: magic, simplicity, and complexity
replies.content:
1) It varies depending on the practitioner. I always used the Rosicrucian approach, though.

2) Depends on the spirit, but yes, it can happen. They can harass you on the astral plane and make it difficult for you to extract yourself from the lower astral even if you would otherwise be able to do so.

replies.title: Re: magic, simplicity, and complexity
replies.content:
2) If I remember right, you've mentioned that sometimes our relationships with spirits can carry over from life to life. If this is the case, then could the spirits continue to harass people who use such methods in later lives?

If this is the case, could it help explain why societies where this kind of magic is practised tend to have so many issues with hostile spirits? Along side the fact that if most of the time when humans approach spirits it's using such methods, spirits could easily learn to associate humans with pain...

replies.title: Re: magic, simplicity, and complexity
replies.content: Good. Yes, very much so. The upside is that as long as there are traditions that treat spirits with respect and kindness, if you affiliate with such a tradition and behave accordingly, spirits recognize the sign in your aura and go, "Oh, he's one of the nice humans. Don't mess him over."

replies.title: Solomonic Magic and its Descendents Re: magic, simplicity, and complexity
replies.content:
To piggyback on this question, is the Trithemian Art of Drawing Spirits into Crystals primarily a method of communicating/harmonizing with spirits, or is this Solomonic magic with a more portable tool set?

If it's not a form of Solomonic magic, what is it derived from?

replies.title: Re: Solomonic Magic and its Descendents Re: magic, simplicity, and complexity
replies.content: It's a different tradition of medieval magic. Notice the tone of the invocations: you don't bully or browbeat them, you invoke God and then politely request a spirit to put in an appearance, then test the spirit to make sure it's who it's supposed to be, and finally send it on its way with a blessing.

replies.title: Re: magic, simplicity, and complexity
replies.content:
". I used to do Golden Dawn magic, which pound for pound has as much hardware and ornate ritual as any magical system in the world. I got good results with it, don't get me wrong -- but I get better results now, with much less hardware and much simpler workings."

comments.title: Norse deities
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
A friend feels drawn to Norse spiritual practice and asked me where she might start. I don't know much about the Norse traditions. She would love it if you could point her to a good book on the Norse runes. She is sort of hoping for a book like your Druidry Handbook where she learned about the Ogham fews. Is there such a book?

replies.title: Re: Norse deities
replies.content:
Hopefully I can offer some help here, as I got started in esoteric stuff via the Runes and have been following JMG's Druidry Handbook in a Heathen religious context for the past year and a half or so.

1) There are many "intro the Runes" books available that different folks find helpful for different reasons, some thoughts:
a. The first thing your friend will have to decide is which "version" of the Runes to use. The short version is that the Runes were first developed before the different Germanic languages split apart, and later languages adapted to the changing languages of the peoples that went to different lands. The most commonly encountered distinctions are the "Elder Futhark", the "Younger Futhark", and the "Anglo-Saxon (Frisian) Futhorc". These names, like alphabet, come from the sounds the first few sounds in the system of writing (f, u, th, a, r, k). If your friend doesn't feel drawn to one of these, I'd recommend the Elder Futhark, as it has the most material written for it as a magical and divinatory system.
b. The site "Norse Mythology for Smart People" has a fairly balanced reading list of 10 books here: https://norse-mythology.org/runes/the-best-books-on-the-runes/ I used this to explore after getting my introduction through Edred Thorsson's books (which feature heavily on this list). These books mostly address the Elder Futhark.
c. If you twist my arm and make me pick one book for using Runes as an overall magical system, I'd say Futhark by Edred Thorsson. If you want a focus on Runes for divination, The Runecaster's Handbook, also by Edred Thorsson, would be my one-stop shop. Both focus on the Elder Futhark and have what seems to me a reasonable balance of interest in historical reconstruction and modern esoteric applicability. If you want a more literal, bare-bones books, my favorite would likely be The Rune Primer by Sweyn Plowright.
d. I don't know as much about books focused on the Younger Futhark, but I've heard that Long Branches by Ann Groa Sheffield is good.
e. The single best source on the Anglo-Saxon Futhorc is the "Anglo-Saxon Rune Poem". I'm a big fan of The Stanzas of the Old English Rune Poem by Gary Stanfield, which is insanely detailed exegesis on the poem, but also provides multiple translations with a discussion of what each reflects from the original.
f. Whichever tradition your friend goes with, the Rune poems (Old Norse, Old Icelandic, and Old English) will provide a good source of meditative material

2) Some blogs/other online resources that might come in handy:
a. The "Norse Mythology for Smart People" blog mentioned above no longer updates actively, but it makes for a pretty decent overview/encyclopedia of the material: https://norse-mythology.org/

As the title might suggest, this site has a bit of a rationalistic/reconstructionist bent that may or may not jive with what your friend is looking for.

b. Depending on what path your friend is looking for, fellow regular gullindagan has been working out a Heathen version of the Golden Dawn and sharing on their dreamwidth here: https://gullindagan.dreamwidth.org/

c. If your friend is amenable to videos, a couple Youtube channels I've found to have some interesting posts are Nordic Animism (https://www.youtube.com/@Runehr) and Scott T. Shell (https://www.youtube.com/@scottt.shellcontinentalger2464)

d. Galina Krasskova has written a handful of books I've found very helpful, and she blogs at "Gangleri's Grove" (https://krasskova.wordpress.com/). I'm not as sure how I feel about her overall, but she at least has good insights on prayer and devotional practice. Likewise with her sometimes co-author Raven Kaldera, who maintains the website "Northern Tradition Paganism" (https://www.northernpaganism.org/)

e. For a broader discussion of practice, the community "Sane Polytheism" has some helpful stuff, and my post soliciting resources has a Heathen focus and can be found here: https://sanepolytheism.dreamwidth.org/3051.html

If you or your friend have any follow-up questions, please don't hesitate to reach out either here, on my dreamwidth, or via DM.

Hope this helps!
Jeff

comments.content:
Looking for suggestions from everyone on creative altar concealment. I've got my magical books in a drawer and this seems to keep them private, but I've struggled with the altar, candles, and incense with my family. I get up around 4-5am to do my practices but if I burn candles or incense there are comments, and I wave them off, but can't figure out how to cancel an altar. How vital is it that I construct each of the magical items in the GD book?

It won't be like this forever in that my adult kids who just ended college will move out sometime in 2023.

Appreciate any creative ideas! Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Altar Struggles
replies.content:
1) A Golden Dawn altar -- Celtic or Hermetic -- is temporary; you don't leave it up all the time, the way some people do with other altars. I use a folding tray for mine, and just cover it with an altar cloth. The altar gear and everything else goes into a drawer when not in use.

2) It's vital. Just remember that you can make them as small as you need to.

replies.title: Re: Altar Struggles
replies.content:
Does the dimension of the folding tray matter? I have a small table that is 18" square surface and meets the square requirement I saw but it's on the small side.

Will get to work on the tools then. I had mistakenly interpreted a response here to another post that the tools weren't that important. I'm glad I double-checked.

Thank you so much again.

replies.title: Re: Altar Struggles
replies.content: As long as you can put everything on the temporary altar you need to have there, no, the size doesn't matter. You could make miniature altar gear, use birthday candles, and have an altar six inches square.

replies.title: Re: Altar Struggles
replies.content:
I am slightly luckier to have a tolerant, if slightly perplexed, wife and kids too little to find such things weird, but I sometimes have family members come stay at the house from whom I desire some privacy. My solution was the Ikea Ivar cabinet (I know, not the best source, but it worked for my budget and availability). I use the top of the cabinet for my altar space (with the addition of a cloth), and put away my religious/magical paraphernalia inside it when not in use. The addition of some open-topped boxes/trays also helped with smaller items.

Good luck, and hope these help!
Jeff

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
John--

Two questions for you this week:

1) In an earlitpost, you noted that the present leadership of Masonry is rather hostile toward it occult aspects. Can you say how or why that has come to be?

2) I recently heard a comment in a spiritual documentary made by a Tibetan nun that "too much learning can get in the way of spiritual progress." I've always considered knowledge and the mind to be the primary vehicles for greater spiritual development. How do you see it? Is the nun's observation accurate?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Most of the older generation of Masons are relatively devout Protestant Christians who go to church on Sundays. That's one of the reasons I chuckle when the conspiracy brigade gets bent out of shape about sinister Masonic Satanists -- it's hard for me to think of any organization less welcoming to Satanists! In the US, Masonry has been a social club for patriotic, Christian, politically conservative men for almost a century now; I've read despairing editorials from the 1920s written by senior Masons of that generation, many of whom were into esotericism, going on about how all the young men who were joining don't want anything to do with the occult dimensions of the Craft!

2) Intellectual knowledge can help but it can also hinder. If it distracts you from paying attention to things the intellect doesn't handle well, it can be a major barrier. It's also very common for people to use intellectual knowledge as a way to hide from direct experience, and of course that's another very serious barrier.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Any ideas as to why the hierarchy of the Catholic Church—including the papacy itself—has so often resorted to lies to discredit Masonry?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes, I talked about that in my book The Secret of the Temple. I think it's partly that the Vatican's main exposure is to continental European Masonry, which is much more political and much more anticlerical, and partly because Masonry inherits certain traditions and symbols relating to the disciplina arcani, the old "inner order" secrets of the Christian church. (If you look up that phrase these days, you'll get claims that it was a temporary thing in the early church, but I have my doubts as to whether that's wholly accurate.) The church thinks the Masons know things they're not supposed to know; the irony, of course, is that the Masons don't know those things any more -- that's all part of the Lost Word, the vanished secrets of the Craft. All we have are inscrutable symbols.
